item.title: Destiny 2: Warlord’s Jailbreak Triumph Guide
item.description:
The Warlord's Jailbreak triumph in Destiny 2 requires you to complete the Rathil encounter while freeing other Guardians from their cages. However, this phrasing might not be the clearest, and it could have resulted in a failed run. If you're struggling to get the Warlord's Jailbreak triumph, then we've got you covered so you understand how it works and why it's not triggering.

You will need a second Guardian for this challenge, so try out some LFGs! The encounter isn't difficult, and I'll explain it below.

How to Get the Warlord's Jailbreak Triumph in Destiny 2

Rathil is the first encounter in the Warlord's Ruin Dungeon. To begin, start the encounter and wait for Rathil to summon an "Imminent Wish". You will then be teleported into a cage above. Now, look to your friend who's also in a cage, and you'll see three Taken Eyes around their cage .

Shoot these three Taken Eyes quickly to open their cage, and they need to do the same to you. If you shoot your own Taken Eyes (even one of them), the triumph will fail. If you're playing as a team of three, it's easier to go clockwise; that way, everyone is shooting the cell on their right.

Now that you're out of your cell, capture the "Imminent Wish", and once captured, wait for the timer to go, and then the damage phase begins. Rathil is fairly weak, but if you can't one-phase him, then remember to repeat the challenge above. You need to free your friend from the cell in every phase, not just one of them .

Destiny 2

Follow
Followed
FPS
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 84 /100 Critics Rec: 88%
Released
August 28, 2017
ESRB
T For TEEN for Blood, Language, and Violence
Developer(s)
Bungie
Publisher(s)
Bungie

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
FX Wiki Wiki

Genre(s)
FPS
Platform(s)
PS5, PS4, Xbox Series X, Xbox Series S, Xbox One, PC, Stadia
Powered by
item.content:encoded:
The Warlord's Jailbreak triumph in Destiny 2 requires you to complete the Rathil encounter while freeing other Guardians from their cages. However, this phrasing might not be the clearest, and it could have resulted in a failed run. If you're struggling to get the Warlord's Jailbreak triumph, then we've got you covered so you understand how it works and why it's not triggering.

You will need a second Guardian for this challenge, so try out some LFGs! The encounter isn't difficult, and I'll explain it below.

How to Get the Warlord's Jailbreak Triumph in Destiny 2

Rathil is the first encounter in the Warlord's Ruin Dungeon. To begin, start the encounter and wait for Rathil to summon an "Imminent Wish". You will then be teleported into a cage above. Now, look to your friend who's also in a cage, and you'll see three Taken Eyes around their cage .

Shoot these three Taken Eyes quickly to open their cage, and they need to do the same to you. If you shoot your own Taken Eyes (even one of them), the triumph will fail. If you're playing as a team of three, it's easier to go clockwise; that way, everyone is shooting the cell on their right.

Now that you're out of your cell, capture the "Imminent Wish", and once captured, wait for the timer to go, and then the damage phase begins. Rathil is fairly weak, but if you can't one-phase him, then remember to repeat the challenge above. You need to free your friend from the cell in every phase, not just one of them .

Destiny 2

Follow
Followed
FPS
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 84 /100 Critics Rec: 88%
Released
August 28, 2017
ESRB
T For TEEN for Blood, Language, and Violence
Developer(s)
Bungie
Publisher(s)
Bungie

WHERE TO PLAY

item.title: Kingdom Come 3's 2027 Release Window Sounds Too Good to Be True
item.description:
As set in stone as it is, it's still hard to believe that Warhorse Studios announced a follow-up to Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 last month. It makes a bit more sense given that it might not be a direct sequel, or that it wasn't even the biggest news story of the studio's reveal stream—the confirmation that it's also working on an open-world Middle-earth RPG is, naturally, a bit of a showstopper. But the detail that's hard to shake is actually unrelated to that incredibly exciting second title, and a bit more involved: the follow-up to Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 is targeting Warhorse's next fiscal year for release, placing it somewhere between April 2027 and March 2028.

That window would put the next Kingdom Come game in players' hands less than three years after KCD2 launched in February 2025. On paper, that sounds like a gift from Warhorse to a fanbase it's kept waiting before—after all, it took the studio seven years to go from the original game to its sequel. That said, a sub-three-year turnaround for a threequel that should (in theory) be bigger or better in one way or another sounds ambitious in a way that deserves a second look.

What Seven Years of Development Actually Built in Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2

For context, Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 wasn't some small game that just happened to take a long time to make. It nearly doubled the map size of the already ambitious, indie-developed original, earned multiple Game of the Year nominations (winning a few, too), and sold over five million copies in its first year. It truly was the product of seven years of singularly focused development, and it showed—in the layered quest design, the authentic Bohemian history on display, and the density of daily medieval life that made the world feel inhabited and involved rather than assembled.

Balance the critic averages

Critical Mass

Pick games to balance the averages.

Time: 00:00
Diff: —

Scales

Multiplier ×1.00
Left
Avg —
Right
Avg —

Results

0
—
More Games

Choose a game

Balance the critic averages
Easy (6)Medium (8)Hard (10)

Critical Mass

Pick games to balance the averages.
Time: 00:00
Diff: —

Scales

Multiplier ×1.00
Left
Avg —
Right
Avg —
Results
0
—
More Games
Choose a game

By the numbers alone, that's clearly a pretty stark contrast to what looks like a three-year development cycle. Warhorse Communications Director Tobias Stolz-Zwilling even acknowledged that timeline directly during the post-announcement community stream—though, credit where it's due, his phrasing was direct enough to be disarming:

"If everything goes right, it comes next fiscal year. Which means you don't have to wait seven years for another Kingdom Come game. That is good!"

The spirit of the statement is hard to argue with, but even so, the conditional at the start — "if everything goes right" — seems like it may end up doing a lot of work. The saving grace is probably that the studio has already built a solid foundation with the first two Kingdom Come: Deliverance games, and if it really wanted to, it could use that as a template for the next rather than building the third entry from the ground up.

Warhorse Is Also Working on an Open-World Middle-earth RPG

What's more, Warhorse is simultaneously developing a fully open-world RPG set in J.R.R. Tolkien's Middle-earth, and the last two games director, Daniel Vavra, stepped into a different role to oversee work on a film adaptation of the Kingdom Come franchise. All at once, these are ambitions that would make even the most experienced, bankrolled AAA developers nervous. Despite numerous triumphs over the years, Warhorse Studios is decidedly not that, and Stolz-Zwilling even acknowledged that "this is new for Warhorse Studios" during the same stream.

A sub-three-year turnaround for a threequel that should (in theory) be bigger or better in one way or another sounds ambitious in a way that deserves a second look.

In terms of games alone, two parallel open-world RPGs from a studio whose reputation was built on the precision and patience of its historical worldbuilding is a logistical stretch under any conditions. Warhorse has grown and evolved considerably—from around 120 employees at KCD 's launch to close to 300 today—but raw headcount doesn't automatically produce the kind of focused craft that made KCD2 feel as considered as it did. It isn't just about labor either, as splitting development across two ambitious projects simultaneously means splitting or doubling down on creative direction, development practice, and so much more.

As intangible as that split can make things sound, the creative assignments behind each project make the challenges more concrete. Prokop Jirsa, who served as lead designer on KCD2 , will head the new Kingdom Come title, which is a reassuring sign that someone deeply familiar with the series' design DNA is steering it. But Viktor Bocan, KCD2 's design director, has been moved over to lead the Middle-earth game, which means the two most senior creative architects of KCD2 are now running separate projects rather than pooling their instincts on one.

What Three Years of Development Might Look Like in Practice

The most pragmatic explanation for how Warhorse delivers a new Kingdom Come open-world RPG in under three years is that it won't be building from zero. Elements like the engine, animation rigs, combat systems, and NPC simulation from KCD2 are already in place—and without intending to be reductive, a new map with a new story built on that existing framework is realistically how a studio compresses a development cycle without abandoning what the franchise does best. The bones are already standing.

But optimism should be tempered with caution in certain cases, and the KCD threequel certainly qualifies, because working from KCD2 ’s foundation runs the risk of leaving the resulting game feeling closer to an expansion than a sequel. That's a fair concern, especially considering how intrinsically linked KCD and its sequel already are: in an interview with GamesRadar, Stolz-Zwilling called KCD2 "the game we always wanted to make." Three years is absolutely enough time for a trusted team to build something worthwhile, but it's a harder sell to convincingly pitch something with the bone-deep density that's defined the series so far in half the time.

"If Everything Goes Right"

Additionally, it's important to keep in mind that, right now, Warhorse's target for the title is Embracer's fiscal year, which spans from April 2027 to March 2028. The games industry's relationship with open-world RPG timelines has enough cautionary examples in the books that "fiscal year target" should probably be read as aspiration as much as projection. It's a historically fickle target that's made even more reliant on things landing upright, given that, as of last month, Warhorse's parent company is mid-restructuring.

The Case for Wanting the Next KCD Now

Ultimately, none of these concerns change the fact that more Kingdom Come , sooner, is an outcome fans would take without hesitation. The developers have done nothing to disabuse fans of their trust, KCD2 left meaningful ground uncovered, and the possibility of returning to that world—regardless of it being a direct sequel or not—before the decade ends makes skepticism feel like a worthwhile problem to have. Nobody is upset about these announcements, but splitting focus between a third game, a film adaptation, and one of the largest IPs on the planet just seems difficult. Essentially, it's a lot of "firsts"—maybe too many for a studio that only recently (though deservedly) skyrocketed into the AAA space.

The most pragmatic explanation for how Warhorse delivers a new Kingdom Come open-world RPG in under three years is that it won't be building from zero.

Whether Warhorse can stick the landing with a new Kingdom Come entry and a Middle-earth RPG developed simultaneously is something the coming months will begin to answer. The studio's track record absolutely earns it the benefit of the doubt, and it isn't a question of whether fans want it; of course they do. But if April 2027 comes and goes without a release, the real question of what was worth rushing and what wasn't will get a lot sharper.

10 /10

Kingdom Come: Deliverance II

Follow
Followed
RPG
Action-Adventure
Open-World
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 89 /100 Critics Rec: 95%
Released
February 4, 2025
ESRB
Mature 17+ / Use of Alcohol, Blood and Gore, Sexual Content, Strong Language, Intense Violence, Partial Nudity
Developer(s)
Warhorse Studios
Publisher(s)
Deep Silver
12 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
FX Wiki Wiki GGMods Mods

Genre(s)
RPG, Action-Adventure, Open-World
Powered by

​
item.content:encoded:
As set in stone as it is, it's still hard to believe that Warhorse Studios announced a follow-up to Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 last month. It makes a bit more sense given that it might not be a direct sequel, or that it wasn't even the biggest news story of the studio's reveal stream—the confirmation that it's also working on an open-world Middle-earth RPG is, naturally, a bit of a showstopper. But the detail that's hard to shake is actually unrelated to that incredibly exciting second title, and a bit more involved: the follow-up to Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 is targeting Warhorse's next fiscal year for release, placing it somewhere between April 2027 and March 2028.

That window would put the next Kingdom Come game in players' hands less than three years after KCD2 launched in February 2025. On paper, that sounds like a gift from Warhorse to a fanbase it's kept waiting before—after all, it took the studio seven years to go from the original game to its sequel. That said, a sub-three-year turnaround for a threequel that should (in theory) be bigger or better in one way or another sounds ambitious in a way that deserves a second look.

What Seven Years of Development Actually Built in Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2

For context, Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 wasn't some small game that just happened to take a long time to make. It nearly doubled the map size of the already ambitious, indie-developed original, earned multiple Game of the Year nominations (winning a few, too), and sold over five million copies in its first year. It truly was the product of seven years of singularly focused development, and it showed—in the layered quest design, the authentic Bohemian history on display, and the density of daily medieval life that made the world feel inhabited and involved rather than assembled.

By the numbers alone, that's clearly a pretty stark contrast to what looks like a three-year development cycle. Warhorse Communications Director Tobias Stolz-Zwilling even acknowledged that timeline directly during the post-announcement community stream—though, credit where it's due, his phrasing was direct enough to be disarming:

"If everything goes right, it comes next fiscal year. Which means you don't have to wait seven years for another Kingdom Come game. That is good!"

The spirit of the statement is hard to argue with, but even so, the conditional at the start — "if everything goes right" — seems like it may end up doing a lot of work. The saving grace is probably that the studio has already built a solid foundation with the first two Kingdom Come: Deliverance games, and if it really wanted to, it could use that as a template for the next rather than building the third entry from the ground up.

Warhorse Is Also Working on an Open-World Middle-earth RPG

What's more, Warhorse is simultaneously developing a fully open-world RPG set in J.R.R. Tolkien's Middle-earth, and the last two games director, Daniel Vavra, stepped into a different role to oversee work on a film adaptation of the Kingdom Come franchise. All at once, these are ambitions that would make even the most experienced, bankrolled AAA developers nervous. Despite numerous triumphs over the years, Warhorse Studios is decidedly not that, and Stolz-Zwilling even acknowledged that "this is new for Warhorse Studios" during the same stream.

A sub-three-year turnaround for a threequel that should (in theory) be bigger or better in one way or another sounds ambitious in a way that deserves a second look.

In terms of games alone, two parallel open-world RPGs from a studio whose reputation was built on the precision and patience of its historical worldbuilding is a logistical stretch under any conditions. Warhorse has grown and evolved considerably—from around 120 employees at KCD 's launch to close to 300 today—but raw headcount doesn't automatically produce the kind of focused craft that made KCD2 feel as considered as it did. It isn't just about labor either, as splitting development across two ambitious projects simultaneously means splitting or doubling down on creative direction, development practice, and so much more.

As intangible as that split can make things sound, the creative assignments behind each project make the challenges more concrete. Prokop Jirsa, who served as lead designer on KCD2 , will head the new Kingdom Come title, which is a reassuring sign that someone deeply familiar with the series' design DNA is steering it. But Viktor Bocan, KCD2 's design director, has been moved over to lead the Middle-earth game, which means the two most senior creative architects of KCD2 are now running separate projects rather than pooling their instincts on one.

What Three Years of Development Might Look Like in Practice

The most pragmatic explanation for how Warhorse delivers a new Kingdom Come open-world RPG in under three years is that it won't be building from zero. Elements like the engine, animation rigs, combat systems, and NPC simulation from KCD2 are already in place—and without intending to be reductive, a new map with a new story built on that existing framework is realistically how a studio compresses a development cycle without abandoning what the franchise does best. The bones are already standing.

But optimism should be tempered with caution in certain cases, and the KCD threequel certainly qualifies, because working from KCD2 ’s foundation runs the risk of leaving the resulting game feeling closer to an expansion than a sequel. That's a fair concern, especially considering how intrinsically linked KCD and its sequel already are: in an interview with GamesRadar, Stolz-Zwilling called KCD2 "the game we always wanted to make." Three years is absolutely enough time for a trusted team to build something worthwhile, but it's a harder sell to convincingly pitch something with the bone-deep density that's defined the series so far in half the time.

"If Everything Goes Right"

Additionally, it's important to keep in mind that, right now, Warhorse's target for the title is Embracer's fiscal year, which spans from April 2027 to March 2028. The games industry's relationship with open-world RPG timelines has enough cautionary examples in the books that "fiscal year target" should probably be read as aspiration as much as projection. It's a historically fickle target that's made even more reliant on things landing upright, given that, as of last month, Warhorse's parent company is mid-restructuring.

The Case for Wanting the Next KCD Now

Ultimately, none of these concerns change the fact that more Kingdom Come , sooner, is an outcome fans would take without hesitation. The developers have done nothing to disabuse fans of their trust, KCD2 left meaningful ground uncovered, and the possibility of returning to that world—regardless of it being a direct sequel or not—before the decade ends makes skepticism feel like a worthwhile problem to have. Nobody is upset about these announcements, but splitting focus between a third game, a film adaptation, and one of the largest IPs on the planet just seems difficult. Essentially, it's a lot of "firsts"—maybe too many for a studio that only recently (though deservedly) skyrocketed into the AAA space.

The most pragmatic explanation for how Warhorse delivers a new Kingdom Come open-world RPG in under three years is that it won't be building from zero.

Whether Warhorse can stick the landing with a new Kingdom Come entry and a Middle-earth RPG developed simultaneously is something the coming months will begin to answer. The studio's track record absolutely earns it the benefit of the doubt, and it isn't a question of whether fans want it; of course they do. But if April 2027 comes and goes without a release, the real question of what was worth rushing and what wasn't will get a lot sharper.

item.title: Steam's Free-to-Keep Promotion Has At Least 2 Upcoming 100% Free Games for July 2026, Plus a Bonus Freebie
item.description:
Steam ’s Free-to-Keep Promotion is shaping up to be a little thin for July 2026, as of this writing, with backend tracking only confirming two full games for the coming month. The silver lining is that Steam will likely add more to the promotion throughout July without backend data necessarily confirming them ahead of time.

For anyone unfamiliar, Steam’s Free-to-Keep Promotion is essentially a publisher-driven giveaway system where a game is made available for free for a limited time (typically around a week, though it can vary). If players claim it during that window, it’s permanently added to their library (no strings attached afterward). In general, not every game that ends up on the Promotion pops up in this backend data with much head's up, but practically every game mentioned through Steamdb's backend data ends up on the Free-to-Keep Promotion before it's all said and done. None of the listed games is available yet, but they will be available at some point in July 2026 .

Steam's Free-to-Keep Promotion Games for July 2026

  • The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante
  • Yet Another Zombie Defense HD
  • The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu - Lost Explorers' Swords Pack

As it stands, Steam's Free-to-Keep Promotion has all but outright confirmed the addition of The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante and Yet Another Zombie Defense HD for July 2026. There is a bonus freebie confirmed for next month, and that's the Lost Explorers' Swords Pack for The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu . Set to release on July 15, The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu is a first-person, Lovecraftian co-op horror game where players search a cursed jungle for hidden treasure, complete with Cthulhu mythology, psychological horror elements, and an emphasis on communication. That said, to be clear, The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu is NOT free on the service, just an in-game content pack via Steam's FTK promotion.

GameRant Quiz

—

QUESTION 1/1

Results

0

—

More Games
GameRant Quiz
Easy (15s)Medium (10s)Hard (5s)
—
QUESTION 1/1
Results
0
—
More Games

Otherwise, fans can expect the remaining games to be given away sometime in July 2026, with more details listed on what each game is here. Of course, it's recommended that you wait before purchasing if you've been considering these games thus far.

The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante

Check It Out on Steam (89% Very Positive Reviews)

The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante is a hardcore-narrative RPG that puts players into the entire life of the titular character. This hardcore narrative RPG on Steam lets players live out their entire life under an oppressive empire, starting as a child in a lower-class family through to adulthood. Similar to the decisions made in Disco Elysium , players must navigate through social rules, political expectations, and religious authority.

Choices matter in this Steam game because that's what the entire game is. Not only do players make those decisions as far as they define Sir Brante, but they also impact the player's role in society, what relationships develop and how they form, and even what opportunities players are afforded later in their life. Overall, it's not the most action-packed addition, but it is steep, hardcore, and challenging, all in unique ways.

Yet Another Zombie Defense HD

Check It Out on Steam (87% Very Positive Reviews)

Whereas The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante is a unique spin on the growing Disco-like game genre, Yet Another Zombie Defense HD is the opposite end of that—such a cliche that its name even points out the humor in that.

It's but one of many zombie games on Steam, where the core premise is simple and straightforward: surviving waves of zombies by building up defenses, stockpiling weapons, and fighting back the hordes. It's got more of a classic arcade gameplay loop, which is even better when played in co-op, so it's worth picking up just for the co-op chaos alone.

What's Free on Steam Right Now?

  • Save 100% on Tell Me Why
    • Available Until July 1 at 10 am PT/1 pm ET

As of this writing, Tell Me Why is free to keep on Steam until July 1. It's been available on the promotion all of June in celebration of Pride Month, and it's a narrative-driven adventure game focused on twins Tyler and Alyson. They return to their hometown in Alaska to decode a painful event from their childhood, as well as their mother's death, and how the two have come to understand those moments.

Upcoming Releases
item.content:encoded:
Steam ’s Free-to-Keep Promotion is shaping up to be a little thin for July 2026, as of this writing, with backend tracking only confirming two full games for the coming month. The silver lining is that Steam will likely add more to the promotion throughout July without backend data necessarily confirming them ahead of time.

For anyone unfamiliar, Steam’s Free-to-Keep Promotion is essentially a publisher-driven giveaway system where a game is made available for free for a limited time (typically around a week, though it can vary). If players claim it during that window, it’s permanently added to their library (no strings attached afterward). In general, not every game that ends up on the Promotion pops up in this backend data with much head's up, but practically every game mentioned through Steamdb's backend data ends up on the Free-to-Keep Promotion before it's all said and done. None of the listed games is available yet, but they will be available at some point in July 2026 .

Steam's Free-to-Keep Promotion Games for July 2026

  • The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante
  • Yet Another Zombie Defense HD
  • The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu - Lost Explorers' Swords Pack

As it stands, Steam's Free-to-Keep Promotion has all but outright confirmed the addition of The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante and Yet Another Zombie Defense HD for July 2026. Set to release on July 15, The Mound: Omen of Cthulhu is a first-person, Lovecraftian co-op horror game where players search a cursed jungle for hidden treasure, complete with Cthulhu mythology, psychological horror elements, and an emphasis on communication. Otherwise, fans can expect the remaining games to be given away sometime in July 2026, with more details listed on what each game is here. Of course, it's recommended that you wait before purchasing if you've been considering these games thus far.

The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante

Check It Out on Steam (89% Very Positive Reviews)

The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante is a hardcore-narrative RPG that puts players into the entire life of the titular character. This hardcore narrative RPG on Steam lets players live out their entire life under an oppressive empire, starting as a child in a lower-class family through to adulthood. Similar to the decisions made in Disco Elysium , players must navigate through social rules, political expectations, and religious authority.

Choices matter in this Steam game because that's what the entire game is. Not only do players make those decisions as far as they define Sir Brante, but they also impact the player's role in society, what relationships develop and how they form, and even what opportunities players are afforded later in their life. Overall, it's not the most action-packed addition, but it is steep, hardcore, and challenging, all in unique ways.

Check It Out on Steam (87% Very Positive Reviews)

Whereas The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante is a unique spin on the growing Disco-like game genre, Yet Another Zombie Defense HD is the opposite end of that—such a cliche that its name even points out the humor in that.

It's but one of many zombie games on Steam, where the core premise is simple and straightforward: surviving waves of zombies by building up defenses, stockpiling weapons, and fighting back the hordes. It's got more of a classic arcade gameplay loop, which is even better when played in co-op, so it's worth picking up just for the co-op chaos alone.

What's Free on Steam Right Now?

  • Save 100% on Tell Me Why
    • Available Until July 1 at 10 am PT/1 pm ET

As of this writing, Tell Me Why is free to keep on Steam until July 1. It's been available on the promotion all of June in celebration of Pride Month, and it's a narrative-driven adventure game focused on twins Tyler and Alyson. They return to their hometown in Alaska to decode a painful event from their childhood, as well as their mother's death, and how the two have come to understand those moments.

Upcoming Releases

item.title: 7 Open-World RPGs That Are 10/10, No Notes
item.description:
There’s no shortage of great open-world role-playing games releasing each year, but very few reach truly iconic status and enter the pantheon of the best of the best, universally beloved by several generations of players. It usually takes more than just repeating what we’ve already seen elsewhere on a larger scale with modern technology, so every truly iconic open-world RPG has its own unique hooks that ensure its lasting legacy.

Some games have set the bar so high that they have remained pretty much unmatched in the genre for years or even decades, with their reputation enduring even today. From true narrative or exploration masterpieces to worldbuilding kings or even RPG combat superstars — here are must-play open-world RPGs for the ages that undeniably deserve 10 out of 10.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt

Unmatched in Storytelling

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt stands as a truly standout achievement in the open-world RPG genre; since then, no other game has managed to match the narrative strength of this fantasy masterpiece. Its lengthy, epic, and branching story, where each new character or questline is as complex as the last, coupled with a multi-regional world of rare beauty and a distinct feel, instantly made The Witcher 3 a benchmark for the genre. Its iconic expansions, each the size of a full-fledged game, did the same for the entire industry.

As CD Projekt Red prepares for the next Witcher trilogy led by Ciri, it’s not over yet for The Witcher 3 . The upcoming Songs of the Past expansion aims to tie both stories together, while ensuring that Geralt’s final chapter remains even more lasting than it has been for the past decade.

Fallout: New Vegas

Unmatched in Roleplay

The oldest game on this list, released almost 16 years ago, is still cited by many fans as the ultimate Fallout game, and it’s hard to argue. While later entries like Fallout 4 might be more flexible and rich in content for sandbox exploration, crafting, and building, Fallout: New Vegas somehow captured the spirit of this universe perfectly. This is evident in how the game has influenced the Fallout TV show, particularly in season 2.

On top of that, Fallout: New Vegas is probably the most RPG of them all, offering countless possibilities for creating and playing as the wildest characters imaginable, with NPCs, quests, and the entire world around you reacting accordingly. While the technological aspect of New Vegas unsurprisingly appears quite outdated for new players today, let’s hope the game receives remake treatment sooner or later, as it deserves it no less than Oblivion .

The Elder Scrolls 5: Skyrim

Unmatched in Flexibility

Speaking of The Elder Scrolls , it’s still impossible not to include the true evergreen icon that is Skyrim on a list like this. It’s really mind-boggling how tens of thousands of players still play the game (albeit heavily modded, most likely) daily, but here we are. Having emerged as a true RPG blueprint with incredibly flexible potential that continues to be tailored to every fan’s personal preferences by modders, Skyrim single-handedly proves that it’s not the graphics or technology that matters most for the genre, but things far more ethereal.

This is even more evident in how Bethesda has struggled to repeat its absolute magnum opus ever since, with both Fallout 4 and Starfield still bound to live in its shadow. Skyrim has long become something more than a game for countless players, and it’s safe to assume that it’ll stay that way for several more years — at least until The Elder Scrolls 6 .

Test your knowledge.

Sign in to claim your place on the leaderboard!

Leaderboard
No scores yet. Be the first.
1/10

Question text goes here?

Results

0 Points

—

Sign in to claim your place on the leaderboard!

Leaderboard
No scores yet. Be the first.
Test your knowledge.
Easy · 15sMedium · 10sHard · 5sPermadeath · 5s
Sign in to claim your place on the leaderboard!
Leaderboard
No scores yet. Be the first.
1/10
Question text goes here?
Results
0 Points
— Sign in to claim your place on the leaderboard!
Leaderboard
No scores yet. Be the first.

Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2

Unmatched in Immersion

The newest addition to the list of open-world RPG masterpieces, Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 takes several confident steps forward in every department compared to the AA original, emerging as a strong GOTY contender. As a result, many players universally agree that KCD 2 represents the ultimate medieval RPG of this generation, fully deserving to stand alongside titans like The Witcher 3 or Skyrim .

Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 is also easily the most immersive open-world game since Red Dead Redemption 2 , delivering its world with uncompromising detail and capturing an authentic feel of daily life. KCD 2 walks this thin line admirably, never resorting to unnecessary busywork just for the sake of it, and instead creates an absorbing RPG experience that allows you to truly embody your character, Henry of Skalitz, like very few titles ever managed.

Cyberpunk 2077

Unmatched in Setting

These days, no one can deny CDPR’s status as the modern kings of the RPG genre, as two of the company’s latest games have earned universal acclaim in the open-world RPG space, even though it took Cyberpunk 2077 some time and work to get there. Nevertheless, despite wildly different settings, camera perspectives, and gameplay feel, both The Witcher 3 and Cyberpunk 2077 essentially share the same strengths: mature themes, complex characters, and memorable hand-crafted quests etched into the memories of every player.

While The Witcher 3 established itself as a true narrative powerhouse that even Cyberpunk 2077 could not match, the latter won extra points through its unique, richly realized setting of a city of the dark future. There’s just no other open-world RPG out there quite like it, so it’s even more interesting to see what’s coming next for the franchise, which is still brimming with potential.

Assassin’s Creed Odyssey

Unmatched in Vibrance

Assassin’s Creed Odyssey is arguably not as acclaimed as the majority of titles on this list, yet it represents one of the best Ubisoft efforts in delivering a true open-world RPG to remember. It’s an alluring and epic journey that’s a sheer joy to play from start to finish, capable of being both lighthearted and impactful when it matters. With a truly stunning world of Ancient Greece ripe for land and sea exploration, approachable and flashy combat, and an array of activities and quests to tackle, the game delivers an unforgettable experience.

Even several entries later, it seems that AC Odyssey is only growing more appreciated, remaining popular among open-world RPG fans and outpacing even newer releases like AC Valhalla and Shadows in active players. What’s more, the game shares neat connections with newer titles in the series through the lasting legacy of its protagonist, Kassandra, further reflecting the game’s impactful status in the entire Assassin’s Creed franchise.

Elden Ring

Unmatched in Combat

The only standalone title on the list of true open-world RPG masterpieces, Elden Ring proves how hard it is to match the long-established franchises in gaming today. Even if it’s technically a standalone game, Elden Ring capitalizes on everything FromSoftware has accomplished in its established genre over more than a decade, feeling like the perfect evolution of the soulslike subgenre as a whole. With all that, it’s even more interesting to see where the studio goes from here.

In every regard, Elden Ring is a standout achievement, blending the best traits of the open-world formula with rich RPG potential — especially when it comes to character building and the countless ways to defeat every boss in the game. Combining refined combat and unmatched depth of exploration with one of the most intriguing fantasy universes ever created, Elden Ring is a true marvel and one of the biggest influences in the medium this generation.
item.content:encoded:
There’s no shortage of great open-world role-playing games releasing each year, but very few reach truly iconic status and enter the pantheon of the best of the best, universally beloved by several generations of players. It usually takes more than just repeating what we’ve already seen elsewhere on a larger scale with modern technology, so every truly iconic open-world RPG has its own unique hooks that ensure its lasting legacy.

Some games have set the bar so high that they have remained pretty much unmatched in the genre for years or even decades, with their reputation enduring even today. From true narrative or exploration masterpieces to worldbuilding kings or even RPG combat superstars — here are must-play open-world RPGs for the ages that undeniably deserve 10 out of 10.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt

Unmatched in Storytelling

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt stands as a truly standout achievement in the open-world RPG genre; since then, no other game has managed to match the narrative strength of this fantasy masterpiece. Its lengthy, epic, and branching story, where each new character or questline is as complex as the last, coupled with a multi-regional world of rare beauty and a distinct feel, instantly made The Witcher 3 a benchmark for the genre. Its iconic expansions, each the size of a full-fledged game, did the same for the entire industry.

As CD Projekt Red prepares for the next Witcher trilogy led by Ciri, it’s not over yet for The Witcher 3 . The upcoming Songs of the Past expansion aims to tie both stories together, while ensuring that Geralt’s final chapter remains even more lasting than it has been for the past decade.

Fallout: New Vegas

Unmatched in Roleplay

The oldest game on this list, released almost 16 years ago, is still cited by many fans as the ultimate Fallout game, and it’s hard to argue. While later entries like Fallout 4 might be more flexible and rich in content for sandbox exploration, crafting, and building, Fallout: New Vegas somehow captured the spirit of this universe perfectly. This is evident in how the game has influenced the Fallout TV show, particularly in season 2.

On top of that, Fallout: New Vegas is probably the most RPG of them all, offering countless possibilities for creating and playing as the wildest characters imaginable, with NPCs, quests, and the entire world around you reacting accordingly. While the technological aspect of New Vegas unsurprisingly appears quite outdated for new players today, let’s hope the game receives remake treatment sooner or later, as it deserves it no less than Oblivion .

The Elder Scrolls 5: Skyrim

Unmatched in Flexibility

Speaking of The Elder Scrolls , it’s still impossible not to include the true evergreen icon that is Skyrim on a list like this. It’s really mind-boggling how tens of thousands of players still play the game (albeit heavily modded, most likely) daily, but here we are. Having emerged as a true RPG blueprint with incredibly flexible potential that continues to be tailored to every fan’s personal preferences by modders, Skyrim single-handedly proves that it’s not the graphics or technology that matters most for the genre, but things far more ethereal.

This is even more evident in how Bethesda has struggled to repeat its absolute magnum opus ever since, with both Fallout 4 and Starfield still bound to live in its shadow. Skyrim has long become something more than a game for countless players, and it’s safe to assume that it’ll stay that way for several more years — at least until The Elder Scrolls 6 .

Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2

Unmatched in Immersion

The newest addition to the list of open-world RPG masterpieces, Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 takes several confident steps forward in every department compared to the AA original, emerging as a strong GOTY contender. As a result, many players universally agree that KCD 2 represents the ultimate medieval RPG of this generation, fully deserving to stand alongside titans like The Witcher 3 or Skyrim .

Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 is also easily the most immersive open-world game since Red Dead Redemption 2 , delivering its world with uncompromising detail and capturing an authentic feel of daily life. KCD 2 walks this thin line admirably, never resorting to unnecessary busywork just for the sake of it, and instead creates an absorbing RPG experience that allows you to truly embody your character, Henry of Skalitz, like very few titles ever managed.

Cyberpunk 2077

Unmatched in Setting

These days, no one can deny CDPR’s status as the modern kings of the RPG genre, as two of the company’s latest games have earned universal acclaim in the open-world RPG space, even though it took Cyberpunk 2077 some time and work to get there. Nevertheless, despite wildly different settings, camera perspectives, and gameplay feel, both The Witcher 3 and Cyberpunk 2077 essentially share the same strengths: mature themes, complex characters, and memorable hand-crafted quests etched into the memories of every player.

While The Witcher 3 established itself as a true narrative powerhouse that even Cyberpunk 2077 could not match, the latter won extra points through its unique, richly realized setting of a city of the dark future. There’s just no other open-world RPG out there quite like it, so it’s even more interesting to see what’s coming next for the franchise, which is still brimming with potential.

Assassin’s Creed Odyssey

Unmatched in Vibrance

Assassin’s Creed Odyssey is arguably not as acclaimed as the majority of titles on this list, yet it represents one of the best Ubisoft efforts in delivering a true open-world RPG to remember. It’s an alluring and epic journey that’s a sheer joy to play from start to finish, capable of being both lighthearted and impactful when it matters. With a truly stunning world of Ancient Greece ripe for land and sea exploration, approachable and flashy combat, and an array of activities and quests to tackle, the game delivers an unforgettable experience.

Even several entries later, it seems that AC Odyssey is only growing more appreciated, remaining popular among open-world RPG fans and outpacing even newer releases like AC Valhalla and Shadows in active players. What’s more, the game shares neat connections with newer titles in the series through the lasting legacy of its protagonist, Kassandra, further reflecting the game’s impactful status in the entire Assassin’s Creed franchise.

Elden Ring

Unmatched in Combat

The only standalone title on the list of true open-world RPG masterpieces, Elden Ring proves how hard it is to match the long-established franchises in gaming today. Even if it’s technically a standalone game, Elden Ring capitalizes on everything FromSoftware has accomplished in its established genre over more than a decade, feeling like the perfect evolution of the soulslike subgenre as a whole. With all that, it’s even more interesting to see where the studio goes from here.

In every regard, Elden Ring is a standout achievement, blending the best traits of the open-world formula with rich RPG potential — especially when it comes to character building and the countless ways to defeat every boss in the game. Combining refined combat and unmatched depth of exploration with one of the most intriguing fantasy universes ever created, Elden Ring is a true marvel and one of the biggest influences in the medium this generation.

item.title: 'It's Kind of Like Band of Brothers or Saving Private Ryan' Gears of War: E-Day Talks 4-Player Co-op
item.description:
Gears of War: E-Day creative director Matt Searcy compared the game's 4-player co-op campaign to the war movies Saving Private Ryan and Band of Brothers . The main story of Gears of War: E-Day will serve as a prequel to the entire Gears of War franchise to date, which will cover the emergence of the Locust Horde and the devastating attacks against the planet Sera.

The upcoming title from The Coalition is the first mainline Gears of War entry since 2019’s Gears 5 . Gears of War: E-Da y will utilize Unreal Engine 5, with enhanced gore and dynamic destruction effects that players will see upon setting foot in the city of Kelona for the first time. Gears of War: E-Day will have refined character movements and more complex level designs than previous entries. Additional changes to the Gears series' iconic active reload system were also made in response to player feedback.

Gears of War: E-Day's 4-Player Co-Op Draws War Film Comparisons

Although Gears of War: E-Day will feature a sprawling city for players to go through, the game's gritty campaign may evoke some Hollywood beats. In an interview with GamesRadar+, Searcy said that Gears of War: E-Day 's main campaign will focus on the Bravo Squad. The four-person squadron consists of Gears veterans Marcus Fenix and Dominic "Dom" Santiago, alongside series newcomers Mags Carter and Lucas Reyes. Searcy said that the choice to make the upcoming title support 4-player co-op was an essential part of the Gears franchise's core DNA. When discussing the Bravo Squad's fight through the city of Kalona, Searcy invoked some of the best war movies of all time as a point of comparison. Searcy said, "It's kind of like Band of Brothers or Saving Private Ryan, where you're following this group and they aren't always going to be in the right place at the right time."

Find the odd one out before the timer hits zero.

Round 1

Which one doesn't belong?

0 / 10
10.0 s

Results

0/5

Best: 0/5

—

More Games
Find the odd one out before the timer hits zero.
Easy (15s)Medium (10s)Hard (5s)Permadeath (5s)
Round 1 Which one doesn't belong?
0 / 10
10.0 s
Results
0/5
Best: 0/5 —
More Games

Searcy said that the return of 4-player co-op to the Gears of War series, which last appeared in Gears of War 3 , was a "huge investment" from the start. Gears of War E-Day 's main campaign can either be played solo as any of the Bravo Squad's members or in an online party of up to four players. 4-player co-op is supported in both the game's campaign and the multiplayer Horde Siege mode. Although Gears of War: E-Day has some hefty PC system requirements, the game will also have modes for handheld devices such as the Steam Deck and the ROG Xbox Ally lineups. Given the comparison to Saving Private Ryan , time will tell how much of an impact Gears of War: E-Day 's campaign will have with fans.

Gears of War: E-Day will support crossplay between Xbox Series X/S consoles and Windows PCs, including the Steam and Xbox Game Pass versions of the game. By comparison, two-player split-screen co-op will be exclusive to the Xbox Series X/S version. Given that Gears of War: E-Day will be available on Xbox Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass, and Xbox Play Anywhere, the barrier to entry is not steep.

Test your knowledge.

Come October 6, Gears of War: E-Day will be one of Xbox's main torch-bearers heading into the 2026 holiday shopping season. It remains to be seen how many new players will take the plunge into The Coalition's hit series.

Gears of War: E-Day

Action
Adventure
Horror
Systems

October 6, 2026
ESRB
Mature 17+ / Intense Violence, Blood and Gore, Strong Language, In-Game Purchases, Users Interact

The Coalition

Xbox Game Studios

Third-Person Shooter, Action, Adventure, Horror

item.content:encoded:
Gears of War: E-Day creative director Matt Searcy compared the game's 4-player co-op campaign to the war movies Saving Private Ryan and Band of Brothers . The main story of Gears of War: E-Day will serve as a prequel to the entire Gears of War franchise to date, which will cover the emergence of the Locust Horde and the devastating attacks against the planet Sera.

The upcoming title from The Coalition is the first mainline Gears of War entry since 2019’s Gears 5 . Gears of War: E-Da y will utilize Unreal Engine 5, with enhanced gore and dynamic destruction effects that players will see upon setting foot in the city of Kelona for the first time. Gears of War: E-Day will have refined character movements and more complex level designs than previous entries. Additional changes to the Gears series' iconic active reload system were also made in response to player feedback.

Gears of War: E-Day's 4-Player Co-Op Draws War Film Comparisons

Although Gears of War: E-Day will feature a sprawling city for players to go through, the game's gritty campaign may evoke some Hollywood beats. In an interview with GamesRadar+, Searcy said that Gears of War: E-Day 's main campaign will focus on the Bravo Squad. The four-person squadron consists of Gears veterans Marcus Fenix and Dominic "Dom" Santiago, alongside series newcomers Mags Carter and Lucas Reyes. Searcy said that the choice to make the upcoming title support 4-player co-op was an essential part of the Gears franchise's core DNA. When discussing the Bravo Squad's fight through the city of Kalona, Searcy invoked some of the best war movies of all time as a point of comparison. Searcy said, "It's kind of like Band of Brothers or Saving Private Ryan, where you're following this group and they aren't always going to be in the right place at the right time."

Searcy said that the return of 4-player co-op to the Gears of War series, which last appeared in Gears of War 3 , was a "huge investment" from the start. Gears of War E-Day 's main campaign can either be played solo as any of the Bravo Squad's members or in an online party of up to four players. 4-player co-op is supported in both the game's campaign and the multiplayer Horde Siege mode. Although Gears of War: E-Day has some hefty PC system requirements, the game will also have modes for handheld devices such as the Steam Deck and the ROG Xbox Ally lineups. Given the comparison to Saving Private Ryan , time will tell how much of an impact Gears of War: E-Day 's campaign will have with fans.

Gears of War: E-Day will support crossplay between Xbox Series X/S consoles and Windows PCs, including the Steam and Xbox Game Pass versions of the game. By comparison, two-player split-screen co-op will be exclusive to the Xbox Series X/S version. Given that Gears of War: E-Day will be available on Xbox Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass, and Xbox Play Anywhere, the barrier to entry is not steep.
